title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Port lockout cost shippers millions ANCHORAGE: City lost $60,000; railroad was largely unaffected. The Associated Press (Published: October 29, 2002) The West Coast dockworker lockout cost the Port of Anchorage about $60,000 in revenue earlier this month. The city-owned port lost dockage and wharfage fees that would have come from shippers Totem Ocean Trailer Express Inc. and CSX Lines LLC., according to port officials. The shippers lost cargo from two vessels each during the weeklong shipping shutdown. "It affected everybody," said port director and former governor Bill Sheffield. Truck, barge and air shipments were boosted to Alaska during the lockout. About 2,500 cargo containers arrive in Anchorage weekly, the majority of which are handled by CSX and Totem Ocean Trailer Express. Th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: October 25, 2002) Environmental efforts rewarded FAIRBANKS -- The state Department of Environmental Conservation has recognized ABR Inc. of Fairbanks for its conservation efforts. The company's $6,000 contribution to help bring solar panels to Randy Smith Middle School earned the Award for Outstanding Achievement in Preventing Pollution. ABR gives daily incentives to its employees for not using cars to get to work. The company also uses environmentally friendly cleaners, recycled office supplies and Honda hybrid cars. Other companies noted for their conservation were: The city of Palmer was recognized by DEC for its sanitary sludge management. Schlumberger Technology Corp. created an Alaska Materials Exchange program to encourage Alaska businesses to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski gets tough ELECTION: Candidate for governor calls for more law enforcement in Bush, tougher judges. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 8, 2002) U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski greeted people before speaking at the Eagle River Chamber of Commerce luncheon Wednesday at the North Slope Restaurant in Eagle River. Murkowski presented his public safety plan which calls for more law enforcement in the Bush, expanding the prison system and updating the state's criminal code. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Sen. Murkowski talked with firefighters at Fire Station 11 in Eagle River on Wednesday. Murkowski outlined his public safety goals during a campaign speech. Next to Murkowski, from left, are Deputy Chief Hal Wiley, engineer Mike Ascone, and firefighter/pa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: October 23, 2002) ANCHORAGE Driver dies after being run over by his tractor trailer in Ship Creek A 27-year-old man died Tuesday when his tractor trailer ran over him in Anchorage's Ship Creek area, police said. Aaron T. Murray was driving out of a parking lot on East Whitney Road shortly before 1:30 p.m. when he noticed the brakes of his rig were sticking. He apparently got out and went to the back of the truck with a hammer. Murray was banging on a tire when something released and caused the rig to move backward, running him over, police said. Police said a commercial vehicle inspector was called in to check the truck. -- The Associated Press ANCHORAGE Comeau hosting meetings to seek community ideas about schools Superintendent Carol Comeau is hosting four c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Wandering polar bear enjoys arctic walkabout DALTON HIGHWAY: Bruin spotted more than 100 miles from its Beaufort Sea home. The Associated Press (Published: October 23, 2002) A polar bear that has traveled more than 100 miles south of the Arctic Ocean along the Dalton Highway stops to check out the bumper of a North Slope tractor trailer. (Photo by Morris Cunningham) Pump station worker Kari Lamothe watched the bear Sunday as it lumbered right up the highway, left. News of any polar bear sightings travels fast on the Dalton Highway, Lamothe said. "If you have a CB (radio) you know where he's at," she said. "He's a pretty hot topic up here." (Photo by Kim Lamothe) Click on photo to enlarge Fairbanks -- A wayward polar bear has made itself at home more than two weeks after it was found wa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Strong quake jolts much of Alaska The Associated Press PALMER, Alaska(October 23, 6:30 a.m. ADT) - A strong earthquake with a preliminary magnitude of 6.2 rocked much of Alaska early Wednesday, experts at the Alaska Tsunami Warning Center said. Major damage is unlikely from the quake, which occurred at 3:27 a.m. Alaska time about 85 miles south of Fairbanks and 170 miles northeast of Anchorage, but it jarred residents in both cities and as far as 350 miles away, seismologist Bruce Turner said. "It's a pretty rural area," Turner said. The most likely damage is broken glass and masonry, he said. Outside Cantwell, a hamlet about 36 miles from the epicenter, the quake roused owners and residents of the Backwoods Lodge. No one was hurt and damage appeared to be limited to "some things falli…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Rescuers call off search BERING SEA: Chances of survival are slim for the three missing crewmembers, Coast Guard says. By Tataboline Brant And Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 23, 2002) The U.S. Coast Guard called off its Bering Sea search late Tuesday for three crewmembers from two different vessels, saying the chances of anyone surviving in the cold, violent water was gone. "We did the absolute best we could," said Capt. Mike Neussl, chief of Search and Rescue for the 17th Coast Gaurd District. The Coast Guard, other agencies and fishing vessels spent more than 50 hours hunting for the lost men. The search for the pair of missing Galaxy crewmembers split into two operations Tuesday morning after a crewmember on a vessel helping in the rescue was washed overboard we…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski says ANWR issue is dead The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(October 18, 3:45 p.m. ADT) - Sen. Frank Murkowski has made it official. Drilling for oil in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge will not be included in a national energy bill, even if Congress can agree on a bill when lawmakers reconvene sometime after the Nov. 5 election, the senator said Friday during a news conference at his Anchorage office. Murkowski, R-Alaska, returned to Alaska after leaving the gubernatorial campaign trail briefly to work with fellow Republicans in Washington on a national energy bill. Murkowski said Democrats who oppose drilling in the refuge won't budge. And he said Sen. Jeff Binghaman, D-N.M., who is leader of the Senate negotiators, has made it clear he won't send any version of the bill back …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cleric voices strong views MINISTER: New UU leader to join rally against death penalty. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 13, 2002) The Rev. Frances Dearman plans to take a stand against the death penalty today, another step on her new path as the minister of Anchorage Unitarian Universalist Fellowship. Her own label for the death penalty -- "obscene" -- shows that Dearman doesn't mince words. When asked what other political issues she feels strongly about, she raised both hands and exclaimed: "Everything!" "When I see the news from the Middle East, I bleed," she says. She doesn't own a car because "I grudge the expense and the responsibility, and I don't want unnecessarily to contribute to environmental degradation." She is "shocked and appalled" at gaps in Ame…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Incumbent, newcomers vie for seat DISTRICT 26: Republican is on a mission to beat House minority leader. District 26 : Candidates Bios By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 19, 2002) Two newcomers with very different goals are running against House Minority Leader Ethan Berkowitz in West Anchorage. Republican Steve Strait entered the District 26 House race with a single mission: Beat Berkowitz. He squeaked past primary opponent Becky Gay by 16 votes in August, but said he was thinking ahead the whole time. "I ran against Ethan since Day One." A 53-year-old who lives in the Turnagain area, Strait owns a media marketing firm that represents rural radio stations. He's running a combative campaign, citing Berkowitz's support of an income tax last session. "This is just …

summary: | | Hummer wreaks rush-hour chaos CRASHES: Driver runs light, hits four vehicles, police say. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 27, 2002) Rodney Udd, who owns the Hummer of Alaska dealership in Anchorage, was treated at and released from Providence Alaska Medical Center on Monday after he drove his Hummer through a red light, hit two vehicles and then sped down the road, where he hit two more vehicles, police said. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge At least two people were injured Monday afternoon after a motorist drove a new Hummer through a red light, slammed into two vehicles and then sped down the wrong side of the road and hit two more vehicles, police said. One car hit by the Hummer caromed into a van, making for a total of six…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Two churches fight over property (Published: October 21, 2002) FAIRBANKS (AP) -- A Superior Court judge has stopped the eviction of one church group by another, saying that just who owns a Fairbanks church and parsonage is still in dispute. Superior Court Judge Patricia Collins of Juneau dismissed a motion to grant the United Methodist Alaska Missionary Conference legal authority to evict the former St. Paul United Methodist Church from the two properties and seize control of them. The conference made the motion as part of a lawsuit it recently filed against St. Paul United Methodist, which was disbanded from the conference in May and now operates without the United Methodist distinction. Collins ruled earlier this month that the request was premature because the dispute about who owns…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Aurora snags Conoco Phillips' Cook Inlet lease NATURAL GAS: Strategy is intended to increase Aurora production by tenfold. By Bill White Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 19, 2002) Aurora Gas said Friday that it bought the 50 percent interest held by Conoco Phillips Alaska Inc. in the Moquawkie area, a new natural gas field on the west side of Cook Inlet. Aurora has also reached an agreement with Anadarko Petroleum Corp. to acquire Anadarko's entire Cook Inlet lease holdings, including the other half of Moquawkie. Anadarko also has about 40,000 acres under lease on the Kenai Peninsula, Aurora said. The purchase prices were not disclosed. The purchases are part of a strategy to increase Aurora's production by as much as tenfold in the next year, said G. Scott Pfoff, company presi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: • Alaska Weather • Alaska weather links Lower 48 Weather Outlook Wednesday 3 P.M. Eastern Wednesday, October 23, 2002 A huge high pressure system positioned over the Dakotas Wednesday afternoon is supplying very cold air for this time of year across the upper Midwest, the northern/central Plains and the eastern slopes of the Rockies. Temperatures are only topping out in the 20s and 30s in many areas. The flow around the high into the High Plains and Rockies is northeasterly, or upslope, and is resulting in a wide area of snow and flurries from southeastern Wyoming to eastern Colorado, southern South Dakota, much of Nebraska and parts of Kansas. Snow amounts in these areas since Tuesday have been generally light, on the order of 1-3 inches, though some higher amounts have been reported. Far…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | In-state gas pipeline plan debated INITIATIVE: Critics say proposal isn't viable; sponsor defends it as route to riches. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 16, 2002) Panelists at a public forum were divided Tuesday on a ballot initiative to create a state-owned corporation to buy North Slope natural gas and pipe it to Prince William Sound, where ships would load the gas for export mainly to Asia. The state and some oil and gas industry players don't favor the initiative, saying it can only detract from efforts to build a more viable gas pipeline across Canada to the Lower 48. The "all-Alaskan gas line initiative" is one of three initiatives on the Nov. 5 general election ballot. Sponsors got the question on the ballot by gathering at least 28,783 signatures from reg…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | FCC grants rural Web exception BUSH:Decision allows residents Internet access through schools. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 4, 2001) Rural Alaskans may tap into their schools' Internet access that sits idle when school isn't in session, the Federal Communications Commission announced Monday. The decision, which applies only to Alaska, could make the Internet far more affordable outside of urban areas, said Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer. The decision will bring parity between urban and rural rates for online service, said Ulmer, who serves on state and federal information technology committees and helped lobby for the change. "There were people with phone bills in excess of $500 per month in our district," said Karl Kowalski, director of instructional technology for …

summary: | | Marauding bear dies on Slope GRIZZLY: State forced to kill sow after break-ins; her three cubs bound for zoos. The Associated Press (Published: August 9, 2002) Orphaned Alaska brown bear triplets arrived at the Alaska Zoo from the North Slope after their mother was euthanized. The two male bears played in a cage as the female bear was prepared for a flight to the Denver zoo. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Two male orphaned brown bears are heading to zoos in the Lower 48 after their mother was captured and killed by Fish and Game staff members. The sow, known as Grizzly No. 001, was well-known in the Deadhorse area. She had been collared since 1991. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Deadhorse -- A well-known grizzly bear was killed in Dea…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Rescuers search for boat MISSING: Shenandoah was towing another vessel in high seas. Coast Guard suspends search for missing boat By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 12, 2002) Amid blowing seas and low visibility, rescuers from the U.S. Coast Guard searched the ocean off the southwestern tip of the Kenai Peninsula on Friday for a missing charter fishing boat and its two-member crew. The search, which turned up debris but no vessel by Friday evening, began when an emergency radio signal from the 48-foot Shenandoah was picked up by the Coast Guard command center in Juneau about 1:30 a.m., said Coast Guard Chief Petty Officer Marsha Delaney. The Shenandoah had been towing another boat, the Mary J, from Homer to Seward through 20-foot seas and winds gusting to more tha…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ulmer chases odds to be state's first woman governor By MIKE CHAMBERS, Associated Press Writer JUNEAU(October 12, 2:45 p.m. ADT) - She's been part of the inner circle for the past seven years, serving as lieutenant governor to a popular two-term governor. But Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer has been waging largely an underdog campaign in her bid to become the first woman elected to the state's top office. And that's with good reason. Despite being Gov. Tony Knowles' lieutenant governor since 1994, Ulmer was unknown to about one fourth of the people in Alaska. Ulmer has battled back from polls that show her trailing Republican challenger Frank Murkowski by as much as 20 percent. With less than a month before voters go to the polls, the two candidates are nearly even. "She's decided to run for offic…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Senate contest offers clear choice SEAT O: Cowdery, Higgins hold conflicting views on fiscal gap and subsistence Candidate BIOS By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 14, 2002) Running for state Senate Seat O in Anchorage are two candidates who contrast as sharply in person as they do on issues like the fiscal gap and a rural subsistence priority. Incumbent John J. Cowdery is a former state representative and a member of the Republican legislative majority since 1996, a retired construction company owner. Cowdery is jowly, congenial, gruff and given to folksy saws, like the ones in his newspaper ads -- "Blaming guns for crime is like blaming beds for sex," goes a recent one, above a caricature of Cowdery looking like an irritated parent. He's 72, facing Patti Higgins, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Forum tackles kids' issues CANDIDATES: Legislative hopefuls answer questions at 'It's About Our Kids!' debate. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 13, 2002) Health care for kids, funding for schools, the quality of child care -- these were the topics debated Saturday during a daylong candidate forum at Wendler Middle School that focused on children's issues. A couple dozen Anchorage legislative hopefuls and incumbents answered questions prepared by organizers during 15-minute windows set aside for each contested race in the "It's About Our Kids!" debate. Several dozen people showed up to listen, some with kids in tow. The candidates said they want Alaska to be a better place for children, but they often disagreed on how they would carry out that vision. For instance,…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alyeska faulted for pace of fixing pipeline issues SHOOTING: Firm says it's developing a better response to spills. The Associated Press Wire (Published: October 5, 2002) Fairbanks -- Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. hasn't moved fast enough in correcting problems brought to light by last year's pipeline shooting and the resulting oil spill, state officials said. "They really didn't get to the heart of the matter," Ed Meggert, head of the Department of Environmental Conservation's northern spill response office, told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. The shooting on Oct. 4, 2001, resulted in a 286,000-gallon spill and a $20 million cleanup effort. Alyeska officials maintain the company has made progress that the state hasn't recognized. Company officials attributed Meggert's concerns to misc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cruise ship officers receive subpoenas RYNDAM: Holland America vessel illegally dumped wastewater. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 19, 2002) Officers aboard the Holland America cruise ship Ryndam have received grand jury subpoenas from the U.S. attorney in Anchorage in connection with an Aug. 17 incident when the ship illegally dumped roughly 40,000 gallons of wastewater into Juneau's downtown harbor. In a financial report this week, Holland's parent company, Carnival Corp., said one subpoena demands that the cruise line produce documents and that Holland America is complying. "If the investigation results in charges being filed, a judgment could include, among other forms of relief, fines and debarment from federal contracting, which would prohibit operations …

summary: | | Steller's sea eagle Tale of big bird catches some air Southwest Alaskans see bird they say is Super Cub-sized (Published: October 18, 2002) Sketch of a Steller's sea eagle (Charles Atkins / Anchorage Daily News) Locations in Southwest Alaska where a large bird has reportedly be seen (Charles Atkins / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Some biologist think the huge bird spotted in Southwest Alaska could be a fairly rare Steller's sea eagle. Habitat: Forests along coastline, large river valleys never far from coast. Range: Nests in Kamchatka and neighboring coast, winters in Korea and Japan. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: October 19, 2002) ANCHORAGE UA plans three workshops on crisis in salmon industry Alaska's salmon industry, once so promising for the state's coastal communities, is now in crisis, challenged by fish farms, frequent poor returns and other problems. The University of Alaska is stepping in to help, university officials said. To address the crisis and focus in particular on the restructuring, quality, marketing and overall health of the salmon resource, UA has planned three statewide workshops and several regional workshops throughout the year. A two-day workshop that's free and open to the public -- "Options for Restructuring Alaska's Salmon Industry" -- will occur at the University of Alaska Anchorage later this month. "Our intent is to augment other efforts ar…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | From tiny tots to hot dogs, ski, snowboard programs abound in the Bowl (Published: October 6, 2002) Whether you're new to skiing and snowboarding or an old pro, dozens of programs exist in the Anchorage area to help you improve -- or just get outside. CROSS COUNTRY SKIING Alaska Winter Stars Coached by former Polish national ski member Jan Buron, this year-round ski program is for recreational and serious cross-country skiers ages 10 and up. Training programs are split into three groups: younger juniors (ages 10-14), high school and advanced juniors (grades 9 and up) and masters. The masters and younger juniors work out five times per week. High school and advanced juniors work out six to nine times per week when high school skiing programs are not meeting. Cost is between $,1050 to $1…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Aviator Joe Crosson soars to hall of fame HISTORY: Museum fund-raiser to honor the Alaska pioneer. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 6, 2002) Pioneer aviator Joe Crosson scored enough firsts during his relatively short career to secure a place in the annals of aviation. He and Ben Eielson share the record for making the first flights over Antarctica in the 1920s. In 1932, Crosson was the first to land an airplane on Mount McKinley. His 1934 flight to Siberia launched airmail delivery to that region. But his most important contribution to Alaska aviation was partly a factor of being in the right place at the right time, said Dirk Tordoff, film archivist for the University of Alaska Fairbanks. "He was in a position to oversee development of the first extensive feeder …

summary: | | Police seek pickup driver in hit-and-run Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 18, 2002) Anchorage police have released more details about the fatal hit-and-run accident Wednesday and are asking for the public's help in finding the driver. Witnesses have told police that a white pickup, possibly a GMC or a Chevrolet, was at the scene when Remy Tacang, 27, was hit Wednesday morning. Tacang's body was found about 8:18 a.m. lying in the right westbound side of Fireweed Lane between D and Eureka streets, police said. Tacang suffered massive head injuries, apparently from being hit by a vehicle, and was pronounced dead at Providence Alaska Medical Center. Police do not know whether the white truck hit Tacang but would like to talk to the driver, they said. Police would also like to talk …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Southwest Alaskans say bird is the size of a small plane The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(October 15, 9:50 a.m. ADT) - Reports of a giant winged creature seen in Southwest Alaska in recent weeks have biolgists and residents puzzled. Villagers in Togiak and Manokotak say the huge bird has a wingspan of about 14-feet - the size of a small plane - and is much bigger than anything they have seen before. Scientists aren't sure what to make of the reports. No one doubts that people in the region west of Dillingham have seen a very large raptorlike bird. But biologists and other people familiar with big Alaska birds say they're skeptical it's that big. A recent sighting of the mystery bird occurred last Thursday morning when Moses Coupchiak, a 43-year-old heavy equipment operator from Togiak, 4…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: October 17, 2002) ANCHORAGE Man killed in apparent hit-and-run; police seek info about accident A 27-year-old man was killed in an apparent hit-and-run Wednesday morning in Midtown, Anchorage police said. Remy Tacang, 27, was pronounced dead at Providence Alaska Medical Center. His body was found around 8:18 a.m. in the right westbound lane of Fireweed Lane, between D and Eureka streets, police said. Tacang suffered massive head injuries apparently from being hit by a vehicle, police said. The vehicle left the scene after the accident. Anchorage police are asking anyone who has information about the accident to phone police. Callers can remain anonymous and be eligible for a cash reward by phoning Crime Stoppers at 561-STOP. Police said they are not investigat…

summary: | | Deal helped state buck the lockout CARGO:Alaska politicians helped increase truck, barge, air shipments. The Associated Press (Published: October 15, 2002) Alaska weathered the West Coast dockworker lockout well, thanks to a deal brokered by the state's political leaders and increased truck, barge and air shipments. Almost all available trucks in the state and Washington were pushed into service during the weeklong shipping shutdown that ended Oct. 4 when the Pacific Maritime Association agreed to hire longshoremen to load ships owned by Totem Ocean Trailer Express Inc. and CSX Lines LLC. The Alaska-bound ships at the Port of Tacoma were being loaded under an exemption to the lockout for 29 ports along the West Coast. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Earnings Briefs (Published: October 16, 2002) GENERAL MOTORS CORP. said it lost $804 million in the third quarter, due largely to a $1.37 billion writedown of the value of its investment in Fiat Auto Holdings and other one-time charges. GM's third-quarter loss equates to $1.42 a share and compares with a loss of $368 million, or 41 cents a share, in the year-ago quarter. Overall, GM sales for the quarter rose nearly 3 percent to $43.58 billion from $42.78 billion in the year-ago quarter. Excluding one-time items, earnings were $615 million, or $1.20 a share. INTEL CORP. failed to meet Wall Street expectations for its third quarter earnings Tuesday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | National group aids Alaska GOP SOFT MONEY: The Republican National Committee gives local party $534,000 for governor's race. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 21, 2002) The Republican National Committee gave the Alaska Republican Party $534,000 this spring to help put a Republican in the governor's mansion for the first time in 20 years, an RNC spokeswoman said Tuesday. "We're in a partnership with the Alaska Republican Party to support candidates and to ensure that we take back the governorship this fall," said Jessica Incitto, a Washington, D.C.-based spokeswoman for the RNC's northwest region. Incitto said Sen. Frank Murkowski's bid for governor is the national party's "primary focus" in Alaska. But the RNC is working "to support all Republican candidates, from t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Thank-yous (Published: October 11, 2002) DIFFICULT TIME, WONDERFUL SUPPORT: The family of Mrs. Matalena Sooalo thanks extended family members, friends and the community for their love, support and prayers through this difficult time. Sooalo died Sept. 8 at Alaska Regional Hospital. Her family appreciates your continued support in the form of a donation, which can be made with any branch of Alaska USA Federal Credit Union, account 1048489. Feresika Bird can be reached in Washington at 1-206-767-4082. Her brothers, George and Sam, can be reached in Anchorage at 272-0911. SISTER CITIES SUCCESS The Sister Cities Commission thanks supporters of the fifth annual Fall Harvest Festival, including donors Mayor and Mrs. George Wuerch, Jeanne Davis, Municipal Light & Power, Davis Black LLC, Arcti…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community datebook (Published: September 25, 2002) BLOOD BANK Blood Bank of Alaska has ongoing needs. Donations can be made 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Monday through Saturday at 4000 Laurel St. Appointments are recommended at 563-3110 in Anchorage, 1-907-260-5672 in Soldotna and 1-907-376-1195 in Wasilla. Current needs include: Condition Yellow ( two-day supply): O-positive, O-negative, A-positive, B-positive. Condition Green ( three- or more day supply): A-negative, B-negative, AB-positive, AB-negative. TODAY SCHOOL LUNCHES Anchorage: Elementary school -- Chicken nuggets with tater tots or pepperoni pizza or chicken burrito, celery sticks, Orange Smiles, cinnamon roll, milk. Middle school -- Hamburger Deluxe, additional entrees, fruits, vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: Chicken Fryz, corn, fresh fr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | President gives ANWR a push MURKOWSKI: Bush says he wants drilling plan included in energy bill. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 26, 2002) Washington -- President Bush called House and Senate energy bill negotiators to the White House on Wednesday, pressing them to finish their work on the bill and restating his support for drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. "The president said, 'I want an energy bill, and I want ANWR in it,' " Sen. Frank Murkowski, R-Alaska, said afterward. The bill is in the hands of a conference committee that is trying to reconcile the House-passed energy bill with the Senate's. The House version would open the refuge's 1.5 million-acre coastal plain to exploration. The Senate bill would keep the refuge closed to development. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoors hot line (Published: October 6, 2002) Web Site of the Week: www.madnorski.org/links.html Want to know about skiing elsewhere? The Madison Nordic Ski Club's Web site provide links to hundreds of Nordic skiing Web sites worldwide. ASTRONOMY Search the starry sky on Friday night Search the sky for stars with the Eagle River Nature Center's astronomy program Friday at 8 p.m. The free program includes indoor presentations by amateur astronomers, then moves to the outdoor decks -- weather permitting -- to search the sky. Bring binoculars and telescopes if you have them. Dress for the conditions. For more, call 694-2108. BIRDING Check Birder's World for primer on bird-watching Birder's World magazine is offering a primer on joining one of the country's most popular activities. Bird-w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | From tiny tots to hot dogs, ski, snowboard programs abound in the Bowl Ski training programs are plentiful (Published: October 6, 2002) Whether you're new to skiing and snowboarding or an old pro, dozens of programs exist in the Anchorage area to help you improve -- or just get outside. CROSS COUNTRY SKIING Alaska Winter Stars Coached by former Polish national ski member Jan Buron, this year-round ski program is for recreational and serious cross-country skiers ages 10 and up. Training programs are split into three groups: younger juniors (ages 10-14), high school and advanced juniors (grades 9 and up) and masters. The masters and younger juniors work out five times per week. High school and advanced juniors work out six to nine times per week when high school skiing programs are not m…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published: October 6, 2002) SNOWMOBILING Annual swap meet set for 11 a.m. Saturday The Anchorage Snowmobile Club has set its annual swap meet for Saturday next to the Enstar Building on International Airport Road between Fairbanks and Eagle streets. Check in is 10 a.m. for people selling gear. Space for sellers costs $20, or get two spaces for $30. Buying and trading run from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Machines, parts, accessories and outdoor clothing are encouraged. The club encourages sales of snowmobile-related items but is not restricting anyone to selling just that. For information, call 566-0272. CYCLING California company touting bike alarm A California company says it is bringing the car alarm to two-wheelers. With more than 1.5 million bicycle thefts reported every year, F…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Man flees Stuckagain fire LOSS: Widower Pete Sloan escapes blaze but loses cherished possessions. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 8, 2002) Anchorage Firefighters, Fire Investigators And Utility Workers Try To Pull Down The Chimney Of A Log House On Near Point Drive In Stuckagain Heights That Was Destroyed By Fire Early Monday Morning. Investigators Say A Smoke Alarm Saved The Homeowner. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge A fire raged through a three-story cedar log house in Stuckagain Heights early Monday morning, destroying a widower's home. Pete Sloan, sole occupant of the house, suffered only minor injuries to his left hand but shed tears thinking of all he had lost. Sloan's wife, Kathy, died last summer of brain can…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Nerve agents tested here FORT GREELY: In the mid-1960s, Army dropped sarin, VX at Gerstle River. By Matt Kelley The Associated Press (Published: October 9, 2002) Washington -- The United States held open-air biological and chemical weapons tests in at least four states -- Alaska, Hawaii, Maryland and Florida -- during the 1960s in an effort to develop defenses against such weapons, according to Pentagon documents. A series of tests in Alaska from 1965 to 1967 used artillery shells and bombs filled with the nerve agents sarin and VX, the records show. The Defense Department planned to release summaries of 28 chemical and biological weapons tests at a House Veterans Affairs Committee hearing today. The Associated Press obtained the summaries Tuesday. The documents did not say whether any…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sexual harassment trial is first under fishing act SEPT. 16: Man is accused of harassing fishery observer aboard trawler. The Associated Press (Published: July 19, 2002) Unalaska -- A sexual harassment case prosecuted under the Magnuson Stevens Fisheries Conservation Act is set for trial in September, according to the National Marine Fisheries Service. Antonio A. Donovo is charged with sexual harassment of an observer on the fishing vessel Dona Martita Aug. 9 through Aug. 15, 2001, according to U.S. Attorney Audrey J. Renschen in Anchorage. The nonjury trial is set for Sept. 16 in Anchorage before Magistrate Harry Branson. The Dona Martita is a Bering Sea trawler owned by Trident Seafoods. "This is an important case because of the level of harassment and because we believe it to be the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | High winds beach barge DIESEL: Vessel carrying 22,000 gallons of fuel is torn free of tug in fierce storm. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 9, 2002) Heavy wind and waves pounded the shore near the Red Dog mine port on Tuesday. The weather snapped a tow line connecting a barge to its tug early Tuesday near the port. Wind-whipped surf pounded the vessel, carrying 22,000 gallons of diesel fuel, onto a gravel beach less than 30 miles south of Kivalina. (Courtesy of United States Coast Guard) Villages on the north and northwest coasts of Alaska, including Shishmaref, above, have been hammered over the last few days by the strong low-pressure system moving down from the Arctic Ocean kicking up hellish winds and rain. (Photo by Tony A Weyiouanna Sr.) Click on photo to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Haida Corp. poised to take 63 acres near Sitka The Associated Press SITKA (September 16, 6:00 p.m. ADT) - A Native village corporation on Price of Wales Island is about to receive about 63 acres of national forest land in Sitka Sound, the U.S. Forest Service said. The Haida Corp. requested the land on Siginaka Islands and the Silver Point and Cobb Island areas as part of the Haida Land Exchange Act of 1986. Sitka native organizations objected to the land transfer citing the land as important cultural and hunting grounds. The Bureau of Land Management suspended the transfer while the Haida Corp. and Forest Service worked on an alternative land selection, according to a story published in The Daily Sentinel in Sitka. Bureau of Land Management spokeswoman Susan Lavin said her agency was n…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Majority of Alaskans pan Legislature move POLL: Education bonds get strong support; most have opinions on ballot issues. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: October 9, 2002) Juneau -- A ballot measure to approve a $236.8 million education bond package had strong support among Alaskans who participated in a statewide poll released last week. The poll also showed nearly 2-1 opposition for an initiative to move the Legislature to the Matanuska-Susitna Borough or Anchorage. In a Dittman Research Corp. survey of 510 households, 76 percent said they were in favor of the education bond initiative while 20 percent of respondents said they were opposed. Only 3 percent were undecided, the poll showed. Asked about moving the Legislature from Juneau, 62 percent of the respondents sai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Candidates back Permanent Fund changes PROPOSAL: Ulmer, Murkowski support amendment to make fund work like an endowment. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: October 9, 2002) Juneau -- Two leading candidates for governor back a plan to ask voters to change the Alaska Permanent Fund. Reacting to news that investments in the fund have depleted an earnings reserve account that once topped $6 billion, Democrat Fran Ulmer said she supports a proposed constitutional amendment to make the $22 billion fund work like an endowment. Under such a proposal, the money used for dividends would come from the value of the fund regardless of how it fared in the stock market. The Permanent Fund Board of Trustees have advocated such a proposal for several years, but the Legislature has not vo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ad links Ulmer to 'enemies' of state SAFE: Group says Ayers, Williams are working against Alaska. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 3, 2002) Deborah Williams says she first learned she'd been declared an enemy of Alaska at the Downtown Rotary Club luncheon Tuesday. "Several people came up to me and said, 'Oh, have you heard those ads? We're so sorry.' " Williams complains that a new political ad broadcast by the group Supporting Alaska Free Enterprise takes an unfair and mean-spirited swipe at her and Jim Ayers, the former chief of staff for Gov. Tony Knowles. The ad targets Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer, the Democratic Party's gubernatorial candidate. It notes she supported President Clinton's nomination at the 1992 Democratic National Convention. It goes on to link Ulmer t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Supreme Court reverses wrongful termination dismissal The Associated Press FAIRBANKS (September 21, 12:30 p.m. ADT) - The Alaska Supreme Court has reinstated a wrongful termination lawsuit of a man who claimed he was formed to quit his job at the Interior Regional Housing Authority. Ted Charles, the former tribal planning director, sued in October 1999. He claimed he was forced to quit because of harassment from other agency leaders. The agency finds housing for low-income Natives. Charles said the harassment included threats and criticism from another employee. He said the agency reduced his responsibility, reassigned his authority to another employee, excluded him from meetings, and fired his relatives, including his daughter, son-in-law and relatives of his estranged wife. A Superio…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski counters ads attacking record EDUCATION Senator says he voted annually to increase spending. The Associated Press (Published: October 7, 2002) Fairbanks -- A broadcast ad campaign launched by a soft money-powered political group says Sen. Frank Murkowski voted against federal education spending numerous times in recent years. But Alaska's junior Republican senator said he actually voted to increase education spending annually in his 22-year U.S. Senate career. And Murkowski's gubernatorial campaign has launched television ads to counter those being paid for by the American Small Business Alliance. The alliance, which says it is not connected with Democratic Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer's campaign for governor, flooded airwaves during the past several weeks with its cartoon depiction o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: 2002 Fishing Guide Stories Click photo to read story Anglers line the banks of the Kenai River below the ferry crossing as they try to intercept red salmon returning to the Russian River. A fairly simple fly-fishing rod and reel can open up a new world for anglers. A pair of anglers weigh their silver salmon at the Little Susitna River public use boat launch. A Cessna 206 heads for the channel between Spenard Lake and Lake Hood during takeoff. Bush planes open up plenty of fishing opportunities to Alaska anglers. Bill Plummer hauls his red salmon to shore after his fishing buddy, Chris Peters, netted it for him on the Russian River. Chumroen Dusuntia watches as his king salmon is weighed and measured during the 2000 Ship Creek King Salmon Derby. The downtown Anchorage derby is just one of …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Mat-Su adopts land-use controls ASSEMBLY: Ordinance is meant to control growth in most populated area. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 18, 2002) PALMER -- After years of trying and failing at similar efforts, the Matanuska-Susitna Borough Assembly Tuesday passed a measure adding more land-use controls to the most populated part of the Valley. The Assembly voted 4-3 in favor of the measure, which requires businesses that generate a large amount of traffic or noise, produce hazardous material, or threaten to contaminate groundwater to get a conditional use permit from the borough. For example, businesses that attract more than 750 vehicles a day or more than 100 during peak rush hours would need a permit. The measure only affects the most densely populated …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: October 6, 2002) HOMER Immunization clinics held after whooping cough cases confirmed State public health officials have confirmed two cases of whooping cough, also called pertussis, in Homer. Health officials held immunization clinics all day Saturday and have met with local officials, health care providers and schools. Alaska's schoolchildren are required to have diphtheria/tetanus/pertussis shots unless they secure an exemption for health or religious reasons. The best way for parents to protect their children? "Get them immunized," said Dr. Beth Funk, an epidemiologist with the Division of Public Health. "Whenever you have a population that's underimmunized, we're always worried." -- Anchorage Daily News ANCHORAGE Solar interference expected to disrupt tel…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Scout gear was strange, but boys still had to have it Craig Medred Outdoors (Published: October 6, 2002) North Fork Ship Creek -- Discovery of an aluminum lid to a Boy Scout mess kit near Bird Creek Pass triggered the pondering on the strange, Boy Scouts of America gear many of us used to carry around in the woods. The Scouts were once a significant American supplier of backpacks, canteens (what we now call water bottles), mess kits, cutlery, bed rolls (what we now call sleeping bags) and hatchets. These items comprised what you might call the basic Scout camping outfit. The backpack was a canvas version of a paper grocery bag with a lid and a couple straps sewn on. The Boy Scout manual offered instructions on how to pack it. The instructions were simple: Put soft objects -- bed roll, …

summary: | | Subsistence Special Session of Alaska Legislature starts May 15 Voters want a voice on subsistence ELECTION Prop. 1 passes in Anchorage by a landslide. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 13, 2002) Anchorage voters spoke loudly and clearly to the Alaska Legislature in an advisory vote at Tuesday's municipal election. Their message: Let us vote on subsistence. With all precincts reporting, nearly three of every four voters backed Proposition 1. A yes vote on the advisory question urged state lawmakers to put a constitutional amendment on subsistence before voters in the November statewide election. "It isn't binding in any way, but it's a message from the largest community in the state," said Rep. Reggie Joule, a Native lawmaker and Democrat from Kotzebue. The resu…

Fishing violations lead to penalties for lodge Natives bow out of meeting SUBSISTENCE: Local priority,' closed doors, guest list are faulted. summary: | | Subsistence Special Session of Alaska Legislature starts May 15 Fishing violations lead to penalties for lodge CITATIONS Agents were allowed to fish despite closures. Fishing changes under fed oversight By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 5, 2002) The coming of federal subsistence management turned out to mean big trouble for the Alaska Dream Lodge. The lodge and its clients wound up with criminal citations -- the first after last summer's switch from state to federal fisheries management. Very poor salmon runs were forecast last year for the Kuskokwim River and its tributaries. Subsistence fishing was being cut back to four days a week. The state cut back sportfishing as well, reducing the daily bag limit on the state-managed upper Aniak River from three king salmon to…

summary: | | Subsistence Special Session of Alaska Legislature starts May 15 Groups to meet on subsistence PRIVATE: Special interest leaders to seek compromise in closed-door sessions. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: May 22, 2002) Juneau -- A special legislative session on subsistence never really got off the ground, but some lawmakers and interest groups say they will continue to work on the issue in private meetings in Anchorage next week. Senate Resources Chairman John Torgerson, R-Kasilof, said he plans to bring interested groups together, possibly as early as Tuesday, to seek a compromise. The Alaska Federation of Natives, the Alaska Outdoor Council, the United Fishermen of Alaska and Alaska Professional Hunters Association are among the groups that will be asked to participate…

summary: | | Forum attempts to repeat successes COMMUNICATIONS: Information technology conference draws experts in other fields. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 3, 2002) A Rural Communications Forum on Wednesday attracted more than the usual telecommunications vendors. Health-care providers, educators and rural leaders also showed up to brainstorm ways to make sure rural Alaska isn't left behind as the rest of the country surfs the Internet. Progress in the last decade has been mixed, and longstanding challenges such as how to pay high startup costs remain. Still, participants found successes that might be templates for other regions and set some goals to improve rural telecommunication access. The forum was offered for the second year as part of a long-running Anchorage i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Landslide triggers flood; 25 Dyea residents evacuate EMERGENCY: No injuries reported as community moves to higher ground. The Associated Press (Published: July 24, 2002) Juneau -- A landslide triggered a flood that forced the evacuation of at least 25 people from the small town of Dyea near Skagway on Tuesday. No one was injured in the town, which has a year-round population of about 20 people, and the water had begun to recede by mid-afternoon, officials said. But an emergency shelter was set up at the Skagway City School and vans were sent to pick up people left in Dyea, said Skagway City Manager Bob Ward. "We have declared a local emergency and are working on a risk assessment," Ward said. "All the residents have been accounted for." The landslide occurred early Tuesday when a 700-f…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 'Blizzard Guy' ends DQ quest here SOLDOTNA: New Jersey man has sampled frozen treat in each state. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 7, 2002) Alan Schmidt digs into a Blizzard in Soldotna, home of Alaska's only Dairy Queen. He has now eaten Blizzards in every state. (Photo by Jon Little / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Alan Schmidt's quest to eat a Dairy Queen Blizzard ice cream treat in every state in the union wrapped up -- where else -- in Alaska. Promptly at 1 p.m. Tuesday, he was ushered behind the counter of the Soldotna franchise, the only DQ in Alaska, where he was allowed to make his very own 16-ounce Heath bar Blizzard with a little cold fudge mixed in. It is his favorite flavor. "It's almost as good as if I made it myself," he said. "Oh, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoors hot line (Published: September 29, 2002) FISHING Snagging now allowed in Cordova harvest area Waters of the Cordova Terminal Harvest Area are now open to snagging. The harvest area includes all salt waters north of a line from Odiak Slough to Stump Point and south of a line from Orca Cannery to Knot Point, including Fleming Spit lagoon and creek. The decision was based on a Department of Fish and Game survey count of 750 silver salmon in the lagoon. That is more than needed for brood-stock. The bag and possession limit is six per day and 12 in possession. For more information, contact state sportfish biologist Matt Miller at 1-907-424-3213. Upriver salmon fishing reopened near Kodiak bays Salmon fishing upriver of the Chiniak Highway has reopened. Open streams include all wate…

Each fall the Park Service conducts a lottery in which 1,600 winners win the chance to drive into the park. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 5, 2002) Democratic gubernatorial candidate Fran Ulmer takes a moment to answer questions for Lumen Christi high school student Robert Borucki after her fiscal policy speech Wednesday to the Eagle River Chamber of Commerce at the North Slope Restaurant. Ulmer addressed a packed room while discussing her ideas for closing the state's fiscal gap. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) (Ron Engstrom / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Democratic governor candidate Fran Ulmer called for new taxes to fill the state's budget gap when the state's cash reserves fall short. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Polar bear gathering leads to trail closure CONCERNS: Barrow village corporation fears tour vehicles could send bruins into town. The Associated Press (Published: September 17, 2002) About 60 polar bears have been seen feeding on the pile of whale bones a few miles outside of Barrow in the past month. The village has closed the trail to the bone pile to prevent off-road vehicles from carrying tourists out to see the bears. It is thought that tourists are disrupting the bears and sending some of them back into town. (Photo by Craig George / The Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge Barrow -- The local village corporation in Barrow has closed the trail to Point Barrow at the end of the road north of the old Naval Arctic Research Laboratory. That's because the trail leads to a recor…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | School District tallies record enrollment GROWTH: Official total of 50,029 students is greatest in city schools' 87-year history. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 2, 2002) The Anchorage School District has more than 50,000 students enrolled for the first time in its 87-year history. As in the past, the official total was recorded Sept. 30. The district tallied 50,029 students who attend its 88 schools that stretch from Girdwood to Eagle River and Chugiak. District demographer Ophelia Dargan-Steed originally predicted 49,950 students would attend classes this year. Several factors impact enrollment, including changes in the military, the economy and growing residential areas. School officials never know how many to expect until kids show up. Last fall, the di…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Commercial fishing group backs Murkowski for governor ULMER: Democrat plans to take her case directly to fishing families. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 18, 2002) A day after the state's top commercial fishing association endorsed Republican Frank Murkowski for governor, Democrat Fran Ulmer said she plans to take her message directly to fishing families. United Fishermen of Alaska issued its endorsement Thursday after interviewing both candidates. The group said it wanted to recognize Murkowski's longtime support for commercial fishing interests and liked his positions on resource development and habitat protection. Ulmer, however, said she was surprised and dismayed that the UFA board voted on the endorsement without bothering to read her replies to a lengthy ques…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Man drowns as waves capsize research boat TWO SURVIVE: The crew members were not wearing life jackets. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 15, 2002) A crew member on a scientific vessel died Tuesday when large waves capsized the 5-ton aluminum launch he was piloting at the southwest corner of Prince William Sound. Two other crew members made it to shore. The dead man was identified as Eric Koss, 30, of Woodinville, Wash. At 1 p.m., the U.S. Coast Guard said, it responded to a distress call from the captain of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration vessel Rainier. Koss had been piloting one of Rainier's six launches while NOAA Ensign Jennifer Johnson, 23, and survey technician David Fischman, 33, were surveying the sea bottom. They were not wearing life…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Ear The Divine Appendage (Published: September 29, 2002) TOO HIP TO HOP ... Darlings, check out "ANWR," a new six-piece band formed to rock and roll in favor of opening the real ANWR. Really. They appear to be serious. Gary Webster , a 21-year state worker, and five other musicians with day jobs have produced a CD single to show the world that "real people support the drilling at ANWR." Rick Rydell -- who else? -- has already played it on his show a couple of times, according to Gary. He swears it's not a put-up job by Arctic Power or the Alliance or anyone, although the group would be delighted if any of the above would help out with expenses. Musically the group's sound is classic rock, although the lyrics by Webster, who plays guitar, and vocalist Connie Wilhelm are not exact…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: September 29, 2002) ANCHORAGE Rape suspect arrested after all-day search of UAA campus Police have arrested a 36-year-old man they say raped a 19-year-old student on the University of Alaska Anchorage campus Friday morning. The victim was released from Alaska Regional Hospital on Friday night. Police charged Joseph Coolidge Jr. with first-degree sexual assault. Coolidge was arraigned Saturday in Anchorage District Court. He is being held at Cook Inlet Pre-trial Facility in lieu of $250,000 cash-only bail and release to a third party. The 19-year-old woman was found unconscious and chilled, naked from the waist down, outside the Allied Health Sciences Building. She was assaulted sometime before 8 a.m. Friday, police said. Coolidge is not a student at UAA and ha…
